speaker: interviewer
text: --Belsen. You had indicated that they were doing experiments. And that's why they kept families together. Can you tell me more about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the only thing I know about this is what I saw in the Holocaust Museum in Washington. They showed us all kinds of weird things that they did, you know, with pregnant women--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you weren't aware of any of this at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, at the time, we weren't aware of this at all. We just found after the war. And, well, a child's life in a place like this, I tell you, we had bunk beds, you know, in the barracks. And there were four tiers. And each tier was-- had hay in there. And that's what we lay on, hay. We had our own covers that we could cover, you know, so that they didn't stick us, you know, the hay didn't itch us and so on. And that's how we lived.

speaker: interviewee
text: And early in the morning, they called us out for revelry, you know, or-- so that they could count us-- how many people died? How many can stand up? People who couldn't stand up, they were rolled away. They were carted away. We didn't know where. And they were probably killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes, we were there in the nude in the cold. It was unbelievable. And the Germans were constantly deriding us and screaming at us. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why would they scream at you?

speaker: interviewee
text: At us, to stand in line, to do this, [GERMAN], you dirty Jew, you this, you that. And were beatings and so on. And-- and it was just so much we weren't even shocked anymore. It was just, you know, just a matter of survive it, not to talk, not to get under some guard's skin, you know, so that we don't get beaten up. And a trip to the latrine, always my mother had to be with me because there were stories of kids falling into the latrine and drowning with horrible pus and all that. And in all this madness, and wild, unbelievable hell, there was this-- I can't believe this. But we made matzoh for Passover. Not we-- I mean, there were a couple of weirdos who kept saints, who keeps bread. And they dried them from the bread crust. They crushed them. They made flour from the bread again. They put it in water. They rolled them. And they made matzoh. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you actually have a Seder?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- well, yes and no. We couldn't. We-- we didn't as much as we could. But-- well, yes, we did have a Seder. Yeah. It's kind of a sawed off Seder, like-- because we didn't have all the ingredients and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we did the praying. We did the talking. And then we did the recounting. And we did the praying that we should be, you know, saved from this hell, and so on. On a daily basis, were people religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There were guys who were--

speaker: interviewer
text: There was daily prayers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Daily prayers, that's right. They even made a-- they even organized a Hebrew school. I mean, how much could a Jewish kid suffer already? Right? [LAUGHS] I mean, we suffered like crazy. Then they give us Jewish school. Oy, I tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what time of day would you attend these classes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always in the afternoon, late afternoon. There was a rabbi who taught us with an old, beaten up [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], you know, the Torah. And then we-- we did something. But most of the time, we ran around.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went around the Ukrainian cooks' headquarters who were cooking for the German officers. And there, they tossed out potato peelings, which we fried, and which we broiled, you know, on sticks. And to them, it was-- in those days, we didn't though they had vitamins. You know? We only knew that it had some starch value. You know? So we ate all that stuff. At the same time, my mother kept the family together and kept them alive. My mother was a dynamo that kept our family sane and together. I don't know to this day how-- What was the daily routine, then, in Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Daily routine, we got up for revelry. They counted us. Then we went back into the barracks. And we stayed there. And we waited for-- for dinner-- I mean, for lunch, because that's the only thing we had. Then we had some slop later on in the afternoon. And then we kept waiting. We would lay around. And we tried not getting sick. People get-- who caught colds, they tried to get out of them. But then people got sick. They were only glad to die. And they were glad to-- we had people dying constantly in the barracks. And then there were always empty spaces. People always got their shoes. Somebody got their shirt. You know, they were plundering all the time. Ah, it was a weird experience. And we kids were playing. We played near the holes where we thought they were-- I think I told you this already, but the corpses, [INAUDIBLE]. We were totally immune to the feelings of even when the corpses were moving, we didn't care. We just played. And we-- it was a beautiful day. And the birds were singing. And we had a great time. We were kids. And we were worried about the Hebrew school already. And-- and people were dying all around us. People were getting skinnier and skinnier. People were losing hope. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any cultural activity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely-- well, no, I don't think there was. I'm trying to think, but no, I don't think so. I don't think there was anything. Everybody was just waiting to die. That's what it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was horribly depressing. But for us kids, it wasn't, because our mothers were there. You see? We were-- and then they started bombarding us, the English. And I heard about Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only roundabout way I heard about Auschwitz was my grandfather said that our cousins-- his-- his grandchildren-- went from a different part of Hungary, went to Auschwitz. Must be nice, he says, because it's near Poland. So it's probably not so strict. And they'll probably let them through. And they'll probably live. [SIGHS] But they didn't live. And from Belsen-- so this went on in Belsen. And then the English started to bombard us. And then they told us that we're going to go again. We didn't no where. They got us up. By that time, half the barracks were empty. And they took us all in this empty shower room-- I mean, a huge room full of shower heads. And we just stood there for four hours in the nude waiting to be showered. And the showers never came. After the war, we found out that Belsen tried to experiment with the Zyklon gas. They tried to gas us. But something went wrong. They didn't get the gas. They didn't-- and we weren't killed. And they then-- they took-- we found our clothes after a while, after looking. I mean, all our clothes were piled up in one pile. So nobody could find anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. We were probably 500, 600, you know, I mean, a lot of people. There-- the rabbi from [INAUDIBLE] was still there with his sons. I remember, they were already very skinny. And we all got somehow dressed. And they put us on this cattle train again, which was interspersed with a regular train. The regular-- this time, it was a little bit easier. For some reason, they didn't have time, because English were already coming. So they were trying to get us out as soon as possible. And we-- I had about four friends. And we took the caboose. We rode in the caboose in the end. And after the caboose, they had a couple of more cars which had tanks on them, and half tracks, and so on. And we were going to Theresienstadt. I-- we didn't know, of course, at that time where we were going. We just knew that we were going. And there was stops all the time, because there were air raids constantly. There were dry runs against us-- dry runs, meaning that Allied fighter planes kept peeling off and coming down towards our train line, and checking us out, and coming down. Never attacking us-- for some reason, they looked. And then they went away. And we kids were watching all this from the caboose. It was, oh, exciting. It was like in-- it was wide cinemascope, you know, when the planes were coming down, getting really close. We could see the pilot's eyes practically. This went on for a couple of days. Then one days, they are doing the same thing. But this time, they started shooting. It was like we were traveling, everything went beautifully. We are talking in one moment. And in the next second, the first shots rang out. And all my four friends died at that time in the caboose. We were starting to run down. They all died. I was-- I was practically flying down the caboose, flying down the-- down the steps. The train was already screeching to a halt. And right beside me, one of my friend's head rolled down. One of the guns took-- took off his head. It just rolled down beside me. I was terror-stricken. I was just standing there, you know, right beside the train. And I couldn't move. That was-- after the first plane killed us all, practical-- I mean, didn't kill me. Then came the next wave. Then came the next plane. It came down, and just came against me. I could see the clatter. And I could see the bullets. And I could feel the bullets whizzing by me, killing people. They were running out. A third of the people got killed. The rabbi and his sons got killed in that attack. My Uncle Harry got injured, you know, in the back.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about losing four of your best friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] It's-- I was totally-- I was flabbergasted. It was like a bad dream. I mean, we were such friends. We were close. We were-- ah, I tell you. And then the wave came, wave after wave. I had about 10 planes shooting at me, almost as if-- and I was just standing frozen beside the train. And not one bullet hit me, no ricochet, nothing splintered off and him me, nothing. I couldn't believe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother was screaming at me, what am I doing? Then by the time they realized-- the planes realize that all the people who were coming out of the train were civilians, and they were probably-- they didn't know it was Jews or anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they realized it wasn't a military train. So then the planes took off. And then we all went back into the plane-- into the trains. The train was on a sort of a rise. OK, so-- it was-- it was on a-- an embankment or something like that. And the people streamed back. We had to leave the dead right there. You couldn't do anything because the train-- the two of the-- the machine people on the train got killed. So they got another couple, or maybe one of the German soldiers took the train into the next station. And then that's when they switched the drivers for the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we got into Theresienstadt in the late afternoon. And then they put us into not barracks, but they had regular houses there, where there used to be a-- a very fancy Jewish work type of a situation, which the Germans were showing to the Red Cross, saying, look how nicely we are treating the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: But by the time we got there, almost all of them were taken to Auschwitz. So all the people in the-- all the people who were showed off in Theresienstadt by that time were gassed. So we were the new people that they took to Theresienstadt, because they didn't know where-- where else to put us. They couldn't leave us out on the fields. They couldn't, you know, they couldn't let us go. So we were in-- in Theresienstadt at the time. And then that's where we experienced-- oh, the hunger by that time was really bad, because we just had nothing much to eat. We were foraging. We were-- The Germans very rarely gave us something to eat. The-- the bed bugs were biting. There were this huge flow of blood. And we were full of lice. And I remember the prayer-- prayer sessions increased. People were praying. And they were worrying. And then one morning, we looked out in Theresienstadt. And-- and we saw on the big opening, the plaza-- call it a plaza, a big opening like a big football field. And they-- they had machine guns set up on top of the buildings. And we though, oh, God, it's-- this is our time. And we prayed some more. Everybody was praying. And my mother was crying. And all of a sudden, we hear a lot of, shooting and screaming, and yelling, and blasting. And-- and the door opens as we are praying. And there's this-- a man comes in with a different uniform. It was not a German uniform. It was a Russian uniform and a red star. And I tell you, it was like people fainted. I mean, they couldn't believe that they were free. You know, they kissed this guy.

speaker: interviewee
text: They asked him, where is the rest? Then the Russian doctors came around yelling at us not to eat sardines, not to eat anything that the Americans give us. The American soldiers came around with chocolates and this.

speaker: interviewee
text: They told us not to eat anything solid because it's going to kill us. A third of the people died anyway, just ate. And they died horribly, you know, with their cramps and everything. And oh, and I remember my grandfather at this time saying that everybody was still hungry. But there were no onions. And my grandfather said to me, oh, if I would just have some onions.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I have to tell you, Hungarians and onions, they go hand in hand. You know? The onions-- you know, we fry up the onions. They we boil up potatoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we mix whatever we fry the onions in with chicken fat, with oil, whatever it is. We mix it in with the potatoes. We put salt on it. And it's one of the greatest dishes in the world. I mean, I don't know if you know about this, but that's what it is. So my grandfather kept dreaming about onions, and kind of worked into my brain about onions. And so we were freed. And we just didn't know what to do with ourselves. We couldn't eat because we were so emaciated that it was going to kill us. So we had to eat very light soups that we were allowed to eat. We-- and then everybody came down the fleck typhus, you know, from the lice and so on. It's typhoid fever. And everybody was sick as dogs. They took us into this hospital and with Russian nurses, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your grandparents and your mother sick as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my grandparents, my mother, my grandmother, Harry, [? Yitka, ?] we were all delirious. I remember the fever and everything. And-- and then we-- we kids, and then we made some new friends. I had another four or five friends. And the fever left us early. OK, so we left the hospital compound. And when we left, we went outside. Right outside the hospital, there was the highway. And on the highway, you could see the Russian tanks moving all the time, the American trucks, and so on. And we hitchhiked. And we went looting with everybody else. I mean, German prisoners were coming home. It-- it was unbelievable what was going on. And how we stayed alive in all this looting, I mean, I-- we were constantly on tanks. And we were eating chocolates from these American soldiers-- and-- unbelievable. And then we went into this factory. We-- we read German. You know, we knew German. And we knew it was a honey factory. Let's go in, have some honey. We don't know how the honey was manufactured. There was this emulsion, this huge vat, you know, this sticky stuff. It was half full. And I climbed up with the help of my friends. And I looked in. And I fell in. Luckily, when I fell in, I fell head over. You know, and I fell in feet first. And I started sinking. And I started screaming. I tell the kids, get somebody! I'm dying here! I'm gonna-- I'm gonna die! I'm gonna drown in this honey, or whatever it was. Then my kids, went up, screamed. This Russian sergeant, the Russian sergeant came in, looked in. And he see what it was. And he got a rope. And I pulled myself out. OK, and he pulled me up, with this rope. And I was sticky with this honey, and this horrible feeling of-- oh. So then everybody, all the soldiers laughed. They had some sort of a celebration that night, you know, with the balalaikas, with the-- with the hand organs and stuff. They were dancing. And they washed me. They gave me some clothes, some, you know, Russian soldiers' uniforms. And ah, and we went on our way the next day. And we were looting. All my friends were stealing gold watches, you know, gold pens, any trinket, anything that shown, anything that was yellow, they stole. And I was also stealing the same thing. I was doing the same thing. And as I went into one of the big apartment buildings, and I went to one of the apartments, and just by chance I looked under this bed. And lo and behold, you wouldn't believe this. But there was 10 kilograms of fresh onions-- you know, the hard ones in this red bag, this red, you know, rope-woven bag. I took this out. And I could think of nothing but my grandfather, and how he loved these onions, and how people, you know, need the onions, and how there's no onions anywhere. And that-- so what I did was I took off all my clothes, all my-- I put this big thing, you know, right in front of me. I put all my sweaters, all my-- all my coats and everything on top. I got rid of my-- all my gold stuff. I couldn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: Kids said, you don't need this. What do you need? You're going to carry those onions? You're crazy. You're nuts. Everything. Well, anyway, I-- I was like this. And on trains, and on the-- on the trucks, I held these onions. And I wouldn't tell anybody what it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And gradually, they stole my pens, and my this, my watches, my everything. So when we finally got home about two weeks later-- I mean, we could have died. I mean, all the nuts going around, and all those people who were still killing. And-- and probably they could have killed us. But how we-- I mean, we just didn't-- we were free. You know, there was no more war. There was-- so anyway, we got home. By that time, the parents, and my grandfather, everybody was well, sort of. And they thought that I died. And that-- and it was horrible. They were practically sitting shiva. They were mourning for me, because figuring that they died.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I came in, they beat the daylights out of me first. Then they kissed me a lot. And they said, Tom, what-- why did you do this to us? I mean, did I get wailed at-- ah. Those days, it's not child abuse. You know, it was love. You know? They beat the daylights out of me. And then they loved me up. And they said, what's-- what's underneath all those clothes? I said, grandfather, I was really getting-- starting to have sense of humor at that time. You know, really, you know, feeling good. I said, let's see what I have here. So I started taking my clothes off. And this onion falls out. And you should have seen the eyes on my grandfather. When he looked at these onions, he says-- he says, you have onions? I don't believe this. I can't believe that you brought onions, with all--

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody's bringing gold, and this, and that. And you brought me the onions. Ah, you're-- I mean, in my grandfather's eyes, I was one of the great prophets of the century. Every Passover, you know, when we-- we recounted these things, he always mentioned, Tommy brought the onions, which was so important in his eyes, and so on. And so the onions were-- he fried up as much onions as he could right away, he says, because everybody's going to smell the onions. They're going to come down and break our door down. And this is what happened. As soon as they started smelling the onions being fried, they broke practically the door down. My grandfather had to give away the onions. And everybody-- everybody fried up the onions. Everybody boiled the potatoes. And everybody mashed the potatoes. Even now I'm thinking about it, you get heart burn immediately, you know, when you get this. Oh, it's unbelievable about these onions.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was the story of my-- about my looting. And we were so happy. And we were waiting to get a train out of Theresienstadt to go home already.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, well, you see, this came-- this was a crisis and a crisis, happening on happening. You know, you can go through your life for two years and nothing happens. You go to school. You do this. Maybe you get a job. You do this. Nothing happens. You know, film-- a film always tells you about the big story, about the big crisis in your life. But with us, it was crisis on crisis. We were gonna go home. Then my kidney started giving me trouble. And then my mother took me to this doctor. And-- and then we didn't want to miss the transport going home. And-- and we were-- and we were-- and then finally when we got on the trains to go home, it was the most wonderful feeling. No cattle cars for us anymore, it was open wagons. I mean, they didn't have any-- they didn't have any civilian wagons for us. But they had open cars. And we opted for the open cars because it was May. It was beautiful. It was warm.

speaker: interviewer
text: You talk about going home. Where were you going home to?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were going home to Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: To your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: To our town-- well, first we went to Budapest, because my uncles-- Uncle Ben and Uncle Marton were still working in Budapest. Marton was in hiding. That's why they couldn't catch him. And Benny was also in hiding. So they were never taken away. And so we all went home to Budapest. And we knew where the jewelry store was that my Uncle Marton had before the war. So my grandfather--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the midtown section. In--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: It did. But I already forgot. I already forgot. The only place I really remember where we lived afterwards was [INAUDIBLE], where there was a theater called the [INAUDIBLE] Theater, and so on. But I don't remember where the jewelry store was. All I remember, we were standing outside. And-- and my grandfather said, you go in, because they were-- they didn't expect us. They thought-- they didn't where we were. They thought we died. They thought-- God knows what they thought. And when they saw us, boy, the reunion that we had was unbelievable-- the food, the reunion. And then we all decided to go back to [INAUDIBLE] and-- with the grandparents, Harry, Lilly, and my mother, and I. And we hired a cab. In those days, we didn't have puncture-free tires. We had tires, you know, with inner tubes. And there was a flat every time. I mean, every 10 kilometers we had a flat. Then we run out of gas. Then we bought gas motorcyclists. And then we-- when we finally got home, it was so beautiful. We-- we went right away to see if anybody found the jewelry that we dug away, because on [INAUDIBLE], my grandfather had a jewelry store.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he-- he-- he hid some of his stock. And well, anyway, we found some of the stuff. And it was great to get back into the old things again. When did you find out about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't find out about-- didn't even really think about my father until 1948, when I discovered that my mother had a boyfriend. And I was very upset. I told my mother that, why does she do this? My father could come home. And it's not done. It's not nice. And my mother told me that there's no hope for my father to come through, because everybody froze when the Russians broke through that line around the dawn in [INAUDIBLE] line. And then I cried for awhile. And then that's when I realized that my father wasn't going to come back. And I was getting older. And my mother put me in a children's home, very religious, [INAUDIBLE] in Budapest, where-- [LAUGHS] where I helped out in the kitchen detail. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we-- every Saturday, they picked up our [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], you know the warmed up beans, and the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] and so on. And this was a huge cauldron of [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] with [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], like an iceberg floating in it, you know? Only the tip is a little bit, but the rest was all underneath.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we picked this up, you know, from the baker. And we stopped under the tree. And the four of us, we looked at this [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] floating in this beautiful mess of [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], you know, that we picked up for 50 kids, don't forget. Make a long story short, we took it back into the kitchen. And there was no [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And there was a big scream, and huge cry out of the kitchen. And they grabbed us. And we were grounded for about a week, I can tell you. We didn't get any [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. We didn't get any of this. We didn't get any of that for a while. So that's the story of [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And then from there, I go to Israel. They took us out-- they wanted to take us to Israel. They wanted to us to make the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] to Israel. And my mother got worried because at that time, you know, when the train-- when the boats were going to Israel, they were being sunk you know, by British, by Turks, by this or that, you know? So my mother said, don't go to Israel. Go with your-- with your uncle. Go to Vienna or go to Paris. And then maybe he'll take you out to Canada, because I'll be going to Canada. And I'll be-- I'll-- you'll meet your mother in Canada. That's what it is. [SIGH]
